quote: Monopolies granted to inventors should be temporary and moderate.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Perpetual patents would hinder rather than encourage improvement.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The progress of opulence follows agriculture → manufactures → foreign commerce.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Policies that force manufactures before agriculture are usually mistaken.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Enclosure of common lands generally increases agricultural productivity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Large farms are more efficient than small ones for grain production.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Small properties encourage intensive gardening and horticulture.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Security of inheritance is necessary for long-term land improvement.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Primogeniture and entails preserve large estates but hinder alienation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Freedom to alienate land would accelerate agricultural progress.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The feudal system of tenure long retarded improvement in Europe.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Money rents are more favourable to improvement than rents in kind.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Long leases at fixed rents encourage tenant investment.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The rise of commerce gradually dissolved feudal dependencies.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The power of the commons in Britain arose from the growth of trade.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A numerous and independent gentry supports both liberty and industry.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Excessive concentration of land ownership threatens despotism.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Moderate inequality of fortune is compatible with flourishing commerce.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Extreme equality would destroy emulation and industry.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Extreme inequality destroys the middle rank necessary for liberty.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The balance of power in Europe favours commerce by preventing universal monarchy.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A universal monarchy would stagnate trade through uniformity and oppression.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Alliances and treaties should aim at commercial as well as political equilibrium.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Economic interdependence makes modern wars more costly and less frequent.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Standing armies are dangerous to liberty but sometimes necessary for security.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A well-regulated militia is the best security for a commercial nation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Heavy war taxes fall ultimately on rent and profits.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: War financed by debt shifts the burden to future generations.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Peace and low taxes are the surest foundations of long-term prosperity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The same general principles of political economy govern all nations, though circumstances vary their application.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: All objects of human reason divide into two kinds: relations of ideas and matters of fact. Only the former admit of demonstrative certainty.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Knowledge of matters of fact never achieves the certainty of demonstration; it rests entirely on experience and carries only probable, not certain, warrant.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The mind never has access to external objects themselves, only to perceptions (impressions and ideas). We cannot step outside our perceptions to compare them with an independent reality.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Belief in the external world is not rationally grounded but arises from natural propensities of the imagination—specifically, the mind's tendency to feign continued and distinct existence for coherent and constant impressions.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The supposition that our perceptions resemble external objects is groundless; we have no means of verifying any such correspondence.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Theoretical knowledge about matters of fact reduces to vivid ideas associated with present impressions through custom and habit; it is not the product of rational insight into necessary connections.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Skeptical arguments against the senses and reason are unanswerable by reason, but nature renders them impotent in practice—we believe despite lacking rational justification.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We never perceive any necessary connection between cause and effect; we perceive only constant conjunction—one event regularly followed by another.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The idea of necessary connection arises not from any impression of the objects themselves but from an internal impression: the felt determination of the mind to pass from cause to effect after repeated experience.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Causal knowledge is entirely founded on experience; there is no a priori insight into what causes what. Any effect is conceivably separable from any cause.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Custom or habit is the great guide of human life; it is what produces the inference from observed conjunction to expected conjunction, not reason.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A single observation of conjunction provides no basis for causal inference; only repeated observation instills the habit that generates expectation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The "power" or "force" we attribute to causes is a projection onto objects of what is actually a subjective determination of the mind.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Causal reasoning is a species of instinct, implanted by nature to serve practical life, not a faculty for discerning real powers in objects.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We can never perceive *why* a cause produces its effect; we can only observe *that* it does so regularly.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: All reasoning concerning matters of fact is founded on the relation of cause and effect; this is the only relation that takes us beyond the present testimony of the senses and memory.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The inference from observed to unobserved cases is not founded on any argument or reasoning process; it is founded on custom operating on the imagination.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The uniformity of nature—the principle that the future will resemble the past—cannot itself be established by argument without circularity; all arguments for it presuppose it.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Probable reasoning differs from demonstrative reasoning in that its conclusions can be conceived to be false without contradiction.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The degree of belief produced by probable reasoning is proportioned to the constancy of the observed conjunction and the similarity of the present case to past cases.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Inference from experience is not a product of the understanding operating by discernment of logical relations but of the imagination operating by association.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We have no rational assurance that our inferences about unobserved matters will prove true; we proceed on instinct and habit, not insight.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Relations of ideas—the proper domain of mathematics and logic—can be known with certainty because their denial implies a contradiction.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Knowledge of relations of ideas is discovered by the mere operation of thought without dependence on what exists anywhere in the universe.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Propositions concerning relations of ideas are intuitively or demonstratively certain; their truth is established by comparing ideas and perceiving their relations.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Mathematical truths concern the relations among our ideas of quantity and number; they do not depend on matters of fact.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The certainty of mathematics arises from the fact that we can define our terms precisely and trace relations among precisely defined ideas.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Geometry is less certain than algebra and arithmetic because it involves ideas of extension that admit of insensible gradations, making exact comparison difficult.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Even in mathematics, complex demonstrations can produce less than perfect certainty because each step introduces a possibility of error that accumulates.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: All knowledge concerning matters of fact is founded entirely on experience; there is no other source.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Knowledge of matters of fact is always probable, never demonstratively certain; the contrary of any matter of fact is always conceivable and therefore possible.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We can never know a priori that one event will follow another; only experience teaches us what effects follow from what causes.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The evidence of memory and the senses provides the foundation for all factual knowledge, but even this evidence is not self-certifying.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Our confidence in synthetic knowledge rests on the vivacity of ideas and their association with present impressions, not on rational insight into the nature of things.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Knowledge of unobserved matters of fact always involves an inference from what is observed; such inferences are governed by custom, not reason.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We cannot justify our factual beliefs by appeal to first principles; the justification bottoms out in natural instinct.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: When I enter most intimately into what I call myself, I always stumble on some particular perception or other—of heat or cold, light or shade, love or hatred, pain or pleasure. I never catch myself without a perception and never observe anything but the perception.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: There is no impression of a self from which the idea of self could be derived; if we have any idea of self, it must be an idea of something that remains invariably the same through time, but no impression has this constancy.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: What we call the self is nothing but a bundle or collection of different perceptions, which succeed each other with inconceivable rapidity and are in perpetual flux and movement.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The identity we ascribe to the mind is a fictitious one, produced by the imagination as it surveys a succession of related perceptions and feigns a perfect identity among them.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Self-knowledge is limited to knowledge of present perceptions; I have no privileged access to a substantial self underlying my perceptions.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Memory is the chief source of the notion of personal identity; it reveals the chain of past perceptions and introduces the relations of resemblance and causation that prompt the imagination to feign identity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The question of personal identity is in some sense insoluble; we can give no satisfying account of what binds perceptions into a single self.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The self is not a substance or substratum in which perceptions inhere; it is nothing over and above the perceptions themselves.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Personal identity is not a real tie or connection among perceptions but a quality we attribute to them because of the smooth transition the imagination makes in surveying them.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The mind is a kind of theatre where several perceptions successively make their appearance, pass, repass, glide away, and mingle in infinite variety—but there is no simplicity in it at one time, nor identity across different times.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The comparison of the mind to a theatre must not mislead; we have no notion of the place where these scenes are represented, nor of the material of which the theatre is composed.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Resemblance among perceptions causes the imagination to move smoothly from one to another, feigning identity where there is only similarity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Causation among perceptions—one perception producing or influencing another—further strengthens the fiction of personal identity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The dispute about personal identity is more verbal than real; it concerns what we *call* the same person, not some deep metaphysical fact.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The self is a useful fiction, necessary for practical life, but not a discovery about the nature of mind.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Passions are original existences, not representations of anything; they contain no reference to other objects and cannot be true or false, reasonable or unreasonable.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Reason alone can never be a motive to any action of the will; it can never oppose passion in the direction of the will. The role of reason is to discover truth or falsehood; the role of passion is to move to action.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Reason is, and ought only to be, the slave of the passions, and can never pretend to any other office than to serve and obey them.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Passions are impressions of reflection, arising from previous impressions either immediately or through the interposition of ideas.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The direct passions (desire, aversion, hope, fear, grief, joy) arise immediately from pleasure and pain; the indirect passions (pride, humility, love, hatred) require additional structure involving a double relation of impressions and ideas.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Pride and humility are directed toward the self as their object; love and hatred are directed toward other persons.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Sympathy is the mechanism by which we enter into the sentiments of others; it converts the idea of another's passion into an impression in ourselves.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Passions can be calm or violent; the calm passions (such as benevolence or moral sentiment) may be mistaken for reason because they produce no sensible agitation, but they remain passions nonetheless.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We do not first judge and then feel; the feeling is primary, and what we call moral or aesthetic judgment is the registering of a felt response.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Custom operates on the mind below the threshold of explicit awareness; we find ourselves inferring effects from causes without any conscious process of reasoning.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The imagination performs its operations—feigning continued existence, bridging gaps in perception, constructing personal identity—without our direction or even our awareness.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Many associations of ideas occur instantaneously and without reflection; the transition from impression to idea happens too quickly for introspection to observe.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The felt determination to pass from cause to effect is often registered only obscurely; we may be aware of expecting an effect without being aware of the mental operation that produces the expectation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Habit and custom establish patterns of inference that function automatically; they do not require conscious rehearsal of past observations.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The mind's propensity to spread itself on external objects—projecting internal impressions onto the world—operates without explicit awareness of the projection.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We may be moved by calm passions without recognizing them as passions; their influence is real but their operation is not conspicuous to introspection.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The mind has a great propensity to spread itself on external objects and to conjoin with them any internal impressions they occasion.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Necessary connection, power, and force are projected onto causes; these ideas arise from internal impressions (the determination of the mind) and are then ascribed to external objects.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We gild and stain natural objects with colors borrowed from internal sentiment; beauty and deformity, virtue and vice, are not qualities in objects but projections of feeling.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The projection of internal impressions onto objects is not a deliberate act but a natural operation of the imagination, occurring spontaneously and without awareness.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We mistake the propensity of the mind to pass from cause to effect for a perceived connection in the objects themselves; we take a subjective determination for an objective necessity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Secondary qualities—colors, tastes, sounds—are projections of internal impressions onto external objects; we attribute to bodies what exists only in the mind.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The vulgar naturally reify their projections; philosophy discovers the projection but cannot eradicate the propensity to project.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Failure to recognize projection is a source of metaphysical error; we populate the world with powers, necessities, and qualities that originate in the operations of the mind.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Men are more governed by their passions than by their reason; they frequently adopt beliefs and perform actions from passion and then seek reasons to justify them.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Reason is often enlisted after the fact to defend conclusions reached on other grounds; it serves to rationalize rather than to guide.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Our causal reasonings are often distorted by passion; we attend selectively to evidence that supports our prior beliefs and desires.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The influence of passion on judgment is pervasive but often invisible; we may sincerely believe we are reasoning impartially when we are in fact rationalizing.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Self-love biases our judgments about our own conduct; we are prone to explain our actions in flattering terms.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The love of system can lead philosophers to force facts into preferred frameworks rather than adjusting frameworks to facts.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Religious belief and moral conviction are particularly susceptible to rationalization; strong feeling prompts elaborate intellectual justification.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We are generally poor judges of our own motives; introspection delivers the motive that flatters rather than the motive that actually moves.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Custom and habit are the great guides of human life, but certain fundamental principles operate prior to all custom: the propensity to believe in continued existence, to form causal expectations, to feel sympathy.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The inference from cause to effect is a species of natural instinct, which no reasoning or process of the thought is able to produce or to prevent.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Sympathy is a fundamental principle of human nature; it is the mechanism by which we naturally enter into the feelings of others.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Self-love and benevolence are both original principles; neither can be reduced to the other.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Curiosity or the love of truth is a natural passion that motivates inquiry for its own sake.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The moral sense is natural to man; we are constituted to feel approbation and disapprobation in response to certain qualities of character and action.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The aesthetic sense is similarly natural; we are constituted to feel pleasure in response to certain forms and arrangements.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: These instincts and natural propensities are the foundation of all human thought and action; reason cannot operate without them, and skepticism cannot extinguish them.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Nature has not left it to our choice whether to believe, infer, and feel; these operations are implanted as part of our constitution.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: By liberty we can only mean a power of acting or not acting according to the determinations of the will; if we choose to remain at rest, we may; if we choose to move, we also may.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: This hypothetical liberty—the liberty to do what we will—is universally allowed to belong to everyone who is not a prisoner or in chains.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A liberty of indifference—a power to act without any motive or cause—is not only impossible but unintelligible; it would make action entirely random.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The feeling of freedom in deliberation is not evidence of an undetermined will; it is merely ignorance of the causes that determine choice.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: When we act, we act from motives; motives arise from character and circumstance. This causal chain does not eliminate freedom but constitutes it.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Freedom is compatible with—indeed requires—regular connection between motive and action; without such regularity, action would be capricious and character meaningless.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The dispute about liberty and necessity has been merely verbal; all parties, when they understand their terms, agree that human actions follow regularly from motives.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: There is a uniform and regular conjunction between motives and actions; the same motives always produce the same actions, the same events follow from the same causes.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The necessity we attribute to causes is nothing but the mind's determination to pass from cause to effect; this same determination operates in our reasonings about human action.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: If we judge actions to be necessitated in the same sense that physical events are necessitated, we mean only that they follow regularly from their causes—not that they are compelled by some external power.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Human nature is uniform across times and cultures in its fundamental operations; history would be unintelligible if actions did not follow regularly from character and circumstance.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We constantly draw inferences about human behavior based on known motives and characters; these inferences presuppose causal regularity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: If actions did not proceed necessarily from motives, moral praise and blame, reward and punishment, would lose their point; they are designed to influence action through the mechanism of motive.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The doctrine of necessity, properly understood, does not degrade human nature or eliminate moral responsibility; it simply acknowledges what all men tacitly assume in practice.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Liberty and necessity are compatible; indeed, liberty without necessity would be liberty without character, motive, or meaning.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: We hold men responsible for actions that flow from their characters precisely because the actions reveal the character; a random action, unconnected to motive, would merit neither praise nor blame.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A man who acts from a bad motive displays a bad character; it is this connection that grounds moral judgment, and the connection presupposes necessity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Punishment aims to influence future conduct through the mechanism of motive; this aim presupposes that motives determine action.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The sense of freedom men feel in acting is not falsified by determinism; it is the experience of acting according to one's will without external constraint.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: What we fear in determinism is external compulsion—being forced to act against our will. But when our will itself determines our action, we are free in the only sense that matters.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A universe in which character did not determine action would be a universe in which persons were not intelligible agents but sites of random occurrence.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: History is the great teacher of human nature; it shows us the same motives producing the same actions across diverse times and places.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The uniformity of human nature makes history possible and valuable; if men were infinitely variable, history would teach nothing.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Historical knowledge is based on testimony; the credibility of testimony must be weighed against prior probability and the character of witnesses.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The historian must exercise judgment about credibility; not all reports deserve equal weight, and extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: History reveals the operation of general causes—climate, culture, government—on human action, but these causes work through the universal principles of human nature.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The superstitious and marvelous elements in ancient history should be discounted; they violate uniform experience and likely arise from the love of wonder.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A philosophical historian attends not only to events but to the causes of events, tracing effects to their sources in passion and interest.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The study of history corrects the parochialism of the present; it reveals that our customs and opinions are not universal but products of circumstance.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The argument from design is the only argument for God's existence that carries any weight; but it establishes at most a cause proportioned to the effect, not an infinite, perfect, or morally good deity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: From a mixed world containing both good and evil, order and disorder, we can infer at most a cause of mixed or limited nature—not the God of traditional theology.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: A priori arguments for God's existence are fallacious; whatever can be conceived to exist can also be conceived not to exist, so no being's existence is demonstrable.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The analogy between human artifacts and natural objects is weak; we have experience of minds producing artifacts, but no experience of minds producing universes.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Miracles cannot be established by testimony; uniform experience against miracles is as complete a proof as can be imagined, and no testimony can outweigh it.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The wise man proportions his belief to the evidence; in matters of religion, the evidence falls far short of what would warrant confident belief.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Popular religion arises not from rational reflection but from fear, hope, and ignorance; it is sustained by passion, not argument.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Religious enthusiasm and superstition are diseases of the mind, arising from corruption of the religious impulse.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Polytheism, not monotheism, was the original religion of mankind; primitive peoples personified the forces of nature and assigned each a governing deity.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Religion arose from the passions—particularly fear of unknown causes and hope for future goods—not from rational contemplation of the order of nature.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The transition from polytheism to monotheism was driven not by increasing rationality but by the dynamics of praise and flattery; worshippers exalt their favored deity above all others.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Monotheism tends toward intolerance because it admits no rival gods; polytheism is naturally more tolerant.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Religions oscillate between anthropomorphism and abstract theology; the vulgar require gods with human passions, while theologians purify the concept beyond human comprehension.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The history of religion shows no linear progress; there is flux between polytheism and monotheism, enthusiasm and superstition, tolerance and persecution.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Popular religion in all ages and nations is corrupt; it represents the deity by the lowest rather than the highest human qualities.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: True religion, if there is such a thing, would consist in rational belief proportioned to evidence and in a morality grounded in human nature, not in superstition or enthusiasm.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Moral distinctions are not derived from reason; reason discovers truth and falsehood, but virtue and vice are not relations of ideas or matters of fact discoverable by reason.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Morality is more properly felt than judged of; we feel approbation toward virtue and disapprobation toward vice.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The sentiment of approbation is a calm passion that we experience when contemplating a character from a general point of view, setting aside our private interests.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Virtues are qualities useful or agreeable to the person who possesses them or to others; these four categories exhaust the sources of moral approbation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The artificial virtues—justice, fidelity, allegiance—arise from convention and social arrangement; they are necessary for social life but not immediately instinctive.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The natural virtues—benevolence, gratitude, clemency—are immediately approved by the moral sense without reference to social utility.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Sympathy is the foundation of moral judgment; we enter into the sentiments of those affected by a character and share their pleasure or pain.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The general point of view—the perspective from which we judge character—corrects for the variations of sympathy due to distance and personal connection.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: There is no moral obligation derived from pure reason; all obligation derives from sentiment, whether natural or reinforced by convention.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Beauty is not a quality in objects themselves but a sentiment in the mind that contemplates them; beauty exists in the mind which contemplates it, not in the object.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Nevertheless, there is a standard of taste; some objects are fitted by nature to produce the sentiment of beauty, and judges who perceive them correctly form the standard.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The delicacy of taste—the ability to perceive all the ingredients in a composition—can be cultivated by practice and comparison.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Good taste requires: delicacy of perception, practice in a particular art, comparison among works, freedom from prejudice, and good sense.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Critics who meet these conditions will tend to agree in their judgments, and their consensus constitutes the standard of taste.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Certain forms and proportions are universally fitted to please; cultural variation overlays but does not eliminate natural aesthetic responses.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: The pleasure of beauty, like moral approbation, is a calm passion; we may mistake it for a judgment of reason because it lacks violent agitation.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Beauty in art depends on the fitness of means to ends, the order of parts, and the expression of sentiment; these qualities please universally when properly perceived.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: Disputes about taste are not merely verbal; one judgment may be correct and another mistaken, though the correctness lies in the perceiver's faculties rather than in the object alone.
source: An Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals

quote: All our ideas are copies of impressions, differing only in their degree of force and vivacity.
The imagination is free to transpose and change ideas, but cannot create simple ideas not derived from impressions.
Memory retains both the original order and position of ideas, while imagination is not restrained to the same order.
Complex ideas may be divided into ideas of relations, modes, and substances.
The abstract idea of existence is the same as the idea of any particular object conceived as existing.
We can form ideas of objects we have never perceived by combining simple ideas from experience.
The idea of God is formed by augmenting without limit the qualities of goodness and wisdom we find in ourselves.
All reasoning concerning matters of fact is founded on the relation of cause and effect.
Experience is the foundation of all our conclusions concerning matters of fact.
We cannot penetrate into the reason of the ultimate causes of nature, and must rest content with experience.
Belief is nothing but a more vivid, lively, forcible conception of an idea.
Without the influence of custom, we should be entirely ignorant of every matter of fact beyond what is immediately present.
All inferences from experience are effects of custom, not of reasoning.
The understanding operates in making inferences from experience without the intervention of reason.
We are determined by custom alone to suppose the future conformable to the past.
No object ever discovers, by the qualities which appear to the senses, either the causes which produced it or the effects which arise from it.
Causes and effects are discoverable by experience, not by reason.
When we see similar sensible qualities, we expect similar secret powers and effects.
There is no foundation for drawing any inference concerning the future from the past, except custom.
The imagination supplies the idea of that power or necessary connection which we feel in the mind.
Our idea of necessity and causation arises entirely from the uniformity observable in the operations of nature.
The supposition that the future will resemble the past is not founded on arguments of any kind.
All probable reasoning is nothing but a species of sensation.
Nature has determined us to judge as well as to breathe and feel.
Belief is more an act of the sensitive than of the cogitative part of our natures.
Objects have no discoverable connection together in nature; we can never presume to infer any effect from them a priori.
Experience teaches us not by any argument or train of reasoning, but by custom immediately.
The more instances we have observed, and the more uniform they appear, the more firmly we believe our inferences.
Probability is founded on the presumption of a resemblance between those objects of which we have had experience.
All our experimental conclusions proceed upon the supposition that the future will be conformable to the past.
There are no ideas which occur in metaphysics more obscure and uncertain than those of power, force, energy, or necessary connection.
We never feel any power or efficacy in our own mental operations.
The first time a man saw the communication of motion by impulse, he could not pronounce that one event was connected with another.
Upon the whole, there appears not, throughout all nature, any one instance of connection which is conceivable by us.
All events seem entirely loose and separate; one event follows another, but we never can observe any tie between them.
The necessary connection between causes and effects is the foundation of our inference from one to the other.
We have no other notion of cause and effect but that of certain objects which have been always conjoined together.
Power and necessity are qualities of perceptions, not of objects.
The distinction between power and the exercise of it is entirely frivolous.
Necessity, if we examine it, will be found to amount to no more than a constant conjunction of objects.
We never can, by our utmost scrutiny, discover anything but one event following another.
The constant conjunction of objects constitutes the very essence of necessity.
When we say one object is connected with another, we mean only that they have acquired a connection in our thought.
Necessity is something that exists in the mind, not in objects.
The connexion which we feel in the mind is the customary transition of the imagination from one object to its usual attendant.
All causes are of the same kind, and there is no foundation for that distinction between efficient causes and causes sine qua non.
We may define a cause to be an object followed by another, where all objects similar to the first are followed by objects similar to the second.
Where a cause operates through a number of intermediate causes, the principal cause is distinguished by being the most remote.
Chance is nothing real in itself, and properly speaking is merely the negation of a cause.
Nothing exists without a cause; the contrary opinion implies that things can arise without a cause.
The production of anything without a cause is contrary to experience.
Every event has a cause, though we may not be able to discover it.
We can never demonstrate the necessity of a cause to every new existence.
The idea of cause and effect is derived from experience, which informs us of their constant conjunction.
When we are once acquainted with the operation of cause and effect, we can extend our experience to all times and places.
Objects have no discernible connection together; all conclusions from cause to effect are derived from custom.
Experience only teaches us how one event constantly follows another, without instructing us in the secret connection.
Motion in one body is regarded upon impulse as the cause of motion in another.
All our reasonings concerning cause and effect are derived entirely from experience.
We cannot reason a priori about causes; experience alone can show the connection between events.
The vulgar confound the constant conjunction of objects with a necessary connection.
There is no object which implies the existence of any other if we consider these objects in themselves.
When we define a cause by contiguity and succession, we define what we can perceive.
The ultimate cause of any natural operation is absolutely inexplicable by human reason.
All our reasoning concerning causes and effects is derived from experience alone.
We cannot know the ultimate causes of natural operations; we must be satisfied with particular experiences.
The powers by which bodies operate are entirely unknown to us.
We perceive only sensible qualities; we cannot perceive any power or energy by which objects operate.
Observation of constant conjunction is the sole foundation of our idea of causation.
There is no impression of the self that remains constant and invariable through our lives.
The self is nothing but a bundle or collection of different perceptions in perpetual flux.
When I enter most intimately into what I call myself, I always stumble on some particular perception.
The identity we ascribe to the mind is only a fictitious one, like that we ascribe to plants and animals.
Our several perceptions are distinct existences and may be conceived as separately existent.
The mind is a kind of theatre where several perceptions successively make their appearance.
There is properly no simplicity in the mind at one time, nor identity in different times.
The comparison of the soul to a republic or commonwealth suggests its nature as a connected succession of perceptions.
Memory alone acquaints us with the continuance and extent of this succession of perceptions.
Personal identity arises from the smooth and uninterrupted progress of the thought along a train of connected ideas.
The relation of causation among our perceptions produces the notion of personal identity.
We have no notion of the self distinct from particular perceptions.
The controversy concerning identity is merely a dispute of words.
What we call a mind is nothing but a heap or collection of different perceptions.
Identity is not something really belonging to these different perceptions and uniting them together.
The understanding never observes any real connection among distinct existences.
We only feel a connection or determination of the thought to pass from one object to another.
The notion of soul, self, or substance is nothing but a collection of qualities united by the imagination.
All our distinct perceptions are distinct existences, and the mind never perceives any real connection among them.
Personal identity is a fiction of the imagination, produced by the resemblance of perceptions.
Morality is determined by sentiment; it defines virtue to be whatever mental action or quality gives a spectator the pleasing sentiment of approbation.
Reason is, and ought only to be the slave of the passions, and can never pretend to any other office than to serve and obey them.
Actions themselves have no merit; the merit lies entirely in the motive.
A virtuous motive is requisite to render an action virtuous.
Virtue and vice are not matters of fact discoverable by reason, but by sentiment.
When you pronounce any action or character to be vicious, you mean nothing but that you have a feeling of blame from the contemplation of it.
Moral distinctions are not derived from reason alone.
The rules of morality are not conclusions of our reason.
Vice and virtue may be compared to sounds, colors, heat and cold, which are not qualities in objects but perceptions in the mind.
Nothing can oppose or retard the impulse of passion but a contrary impulse.
Reason alone can never be a motive to any action of the will.
Reason alone can never produce any action or give rise to volition.
The ultimate ends of human actions can never be accounted for by reason, but recommend themselves entirely to the sentiments.
It is not contrary to reason to prefer the destruction of the whole world to the scratching of my finger.
Reason is utterly impotent in moral matters without the assistance of some other principle.
The distinction of vice and virtue is not founded merely on the relations of objects.
Justice is an artificial virtue arising from the circumstances and necessity of mankind.
The rules of equity or justice depend entirely on the particular state and condition in which men are placed.
The sense of justice arises from artifice and human conventions.
The origin of justice is to be found in the selfishness and confined generosity of men.
Justice is acknowledged to be useful to society, and this is the sole foundation of its merit.
The convention for the distinction of property is of all circumstances the most necessary to the establishment of human society.
Property is nothing but those goods whose constant possession is established by the laws of society.
The stability of possession is the most necessary of all social conventions.
Promises have no force antecedent to human conventions.
The obligation of promises arises entirely from the conventions of men.
Allegiance to government is founded entirely on the opinion of advantage which we reap from it.
The duty of allegiance is grounded on the obligation of promises only when promises are explicit.
Benevolence is approved because it promotes the good of mankind.
We approve of justice because it tends to promote the good of society.
Sympathy is the chief source of moral distinctions.
The moral sentiments must be allowed to have a considerable influence on all our judgments of this nature.
Approbation or blame is nothing but a fainter and more imperceptible love or hatred.
Personal merit consists entirely of the possession of mental qualities useful or agreeable to ourselves or others.
Actions are only so far virtuous as they are the signs of some quality in the mind.
Virtue is any quality of the mind agreeable to or approved of by everyone who considers or contemplates it.
We never approve of any sentiment in others of which we have not at least the seeds in ourselves.
The social virtues of humanity and benevolence exert their influence immediately by a direct tendency.
Pride is a pleasant sensation and humility a painful one, arising from a view of our own qualities and circumstances.
The causes of pride and humility must be related to self and independently produce pleasure or pain.
Love and hatred are simple and original impressions, not derived from any prior impression.
Sympathy is the communication of sentiments from one person to another by the imagination.
By sympathy we enter into the sentiments of others and partake of their pleasures and uneasiness.
The passions are divided into direct and indirect, depending on whether they arise immediately from good or evil.
Direct passions such as desire, aversion, grief, joy, hope, and fear arise immediately from good or evil.
Indirect passions such as pride, humility, ambition, vanity, love, hatred arise from a double relation of ideas and impressions.
The object of pride is always the self; the causes of pride are various.
We naturally love what is related to ourselves and hate what is contrary to our interest.
The passion of anger arises from injury or injustice done to ourselves or those we love.
Malice is the unprovoked desire of producing evil to another in order to reap a pleasure from the comparison.
Envy arises from a comparison of ourselves with others and a sense of inferiority.
Pity is a concern for and sympathy with the misery of others.
The idea of the suffering of others is converted into an impression by sympathy.
Respect is a mixture of love and humility; contempt a mixture of hatred and pride.
The will is nothing but the internal impression we feel when we knowingly give rise to any new motion of our body or new perception of our mind.
The immediate effects of pain and pleasure are desire and aversion.
Hope and fear arise from the probability of good and evil.
The calm passions are often confounded with the operations of reason.
Violent passions have a more sensible effect on the mind than the calm.
Strength of mind implies the prevalence of the calm passions above the violent.
Custom and repetition increase the violence of the active passions while diminishing the pleasurable.
The love of fame is a passion which operates on the mind with a peculiar force.
Curiosity is the love of truth and the pursuit of knowledge for its own sake.
The passion of wonder arises from something unexpected or unusual.
The passions are original existences; they contain no representative quality.
A passion can never be called unreasonable except when founded on a false supposition.
The combat of passion and reason is only a figure of speech; passions never truly oppose reason.
Only a contrary passion can oppose or control a passion, never reason alone.
All the objects of human reason or inquiry may be divided into relations of ideas and matters of fact.
Matters of fact, which are the second objects of human reason, are not ascertained in the same manner as relations of ideas.
The contrary of every matter of fact is still possible, because it can never imply a contradiction.
All reasonings concerning matters of fact are founded on the relation of cause and effect.
By means of that relation alone we can go beyond the evidence of our memory and senses.
If we proceed not upon some fact present to the memory or senses, our reasonings would be merely hypothetical.
The ultimate cause of any natural operation is absolutely inexplicable.
Human reason can give no satisfactory answer to the most fundamental questions about nature.
All the philosophy in the world, and all the religion, could never carry us beyond the usual course of experience.
Nature has kept us at a great distance from all her secrets and has afforded us only the knowledge of a few superficial qualities of objects.
The most perfect philosophy of the natural kind only staves off our ignorance a little longer.
Philosophy would render us entirely Pyrrhonian were not nature too strong for it.
Nature breaks the force of all skeptical arguments and keeps them from having any considerable influence on the understanding.
Skeptical reasonings, if they could prevail, would subvert all speculation and all action.
The great subverter of excessive skepticism is action and employment and the occupations of common life.
A Pyrrhonian cannot expect that his philosophy will have any constant influence on the mind.
All our reasonings concerning causes and effects are derived from nothing but custom.
After we have experience of the operation of cause and effect, our conclusions from that experience are not founded on reasoning.
The skeptic still continues to reason and believe, though he asserts that he cannot defend his reason by reason.
A mitigated skepticism or academic philosophy is both durable and useful.
Philosophical skepticism leads us to modesty and reserve in our opinions.
A small degree of Pyrrhonism might abate our presumption and our prejudice.
The limitation of our inquiries to such subjects as are best adapted to our faculties is the result of skeptical reflection.
Divinity or theology has a foundation in reason, only so far as it is supported by experience.
Our most holy religion is founded on faith, not on reason.
Common life is the corrective to excessive philosophizing.
In all the incidents of life we ought still to preserve our skepticism.
I am ready to throw all my books and papers into the fire when I reflect on the weakness of human reason.
The intense view of these manifold contradictions and imperfections in human reason has so wrought upon me and heated my brain.

A miracle is a violation of the laws of nature; and as a firm and unalterable experience has established these laws, the proof against a miracle is as entire as any argument from experience can possibly be imagined.
No testimony is sufficient to establish a miracle unless the testimony be of such a kind that its falsehood would be more miraculous than the fact which it endeavors to establish.
There is not to be found in all history any miracle attested by a sufficient number of men of such unquestioned good sense, education, and learning.
The passion of surprise and wonder arising from miracles, being an agreeable emotion, gives a sensible tendency towards the belief of those events.
The many instances of forged miracles and prophecies and supernatural events prove the strong propensity of mankind to the extraordinary and marvelous.
It forms a strong presumption against all supernatural and miraculous relations that they are observed chiefly to abound among ignorant and barbarous nations.
Upon the whole, we may conclude that the Christian religion not only was at first attended with miracles, but even at this day cannot be believed by any reasonable person without one.
The design argument is the only theological argument with any degree of force.
The cause ought only to be proportioned to the effect; we ought never to ascribe to it any qualities but what are exactly sufficient to produce the effect.
If we infer any particular intelligent cause from the order of the world, we ought never to assign to it any attributes beyond what appear in the effect.
The religious hypothesis affords no inference that affects human life or can be the source of any action or forbearance.
The Deity is known to us only by his productions and is a single being in the universe, not comprehended under any species or genus.
It is uncertain whether the universe proceeds from any cause resembling human intelligence.
From comparing the works of nature with the works of art, we cannot prove by reason the existence of a deity with any certainty.
The existence of evil in the world is inconsistent with the existence of an infinitely good and powerful deity.

If the whole of natural theology resolves itself into one simple proposition, that the cause of order in the universe probably bears some remote analogy to human intelligence, it can afford no inference that affects human life.
Is the world, considered in general, and as it appears to us in this life, different from what a man would beforehand expect from a very powerful, wise, and benevolent deity?
The original source of all things is entirely indifferent to all these principles, and has no more regard to good above ill than to heat above cold.
True religion consists in the practice of all moral duties and a genuine piety.
Superstition and enthusiasm are the corruptions of true religion.
By liberty we can only mean a power of acting or not acting, according to the determinations of the will.
Liberty, when opposed to necessity, is the same thing with chance, which is universally allowed to have no existence.
All mankind have ever agreed in the doctrine both of necessity and of liberty, according to any reasonable sense of these terms.
It is universally allowed that matter is actuated by a necessary force in all its operations.
The same necessity may be extended to the actions of the mind; the conjunction between motives and voluntary actions is as regular and uniform as between cause and effect in nature.
There is no foundation for the distinction between moral and physical necessity.
Necessity is essential to moral responsibility; without necessity, actions could not be praised or blamed.
Liberty of indifference, if it existed, would destroy moral responsibility by rendering actions entirely random.
Men have always agreed that actions proceeding from a constant character or fixed purpose are more to be praised or blamed.
A man is not responsible for actions that do not proceed from his established character.
Necessity and liberty are not only consistent with each other, but absolutely essential to morality.
All men have always agreed in the doctrine of necessity with respect to human actions as well as physical events.
Actions are by their very nature temporary and perishing; it is only their influence on the mind that remains.
We impute actions to persons as proofs of the existence of certain qualities in the mind.
If there were no regular connection between character and action, men could not be made responsible for their conduct.
The dispute concerning liberty and necessity is entirely verbal when the terms are properly defined.
We feel that our actions are subject to our will on most occasions.
This hypothetical liberty is universally allowed to belong to everyone who is not a prisoner and in chains.
Where there is no free power of acting, there is no merit or demerit.
Actions that proceed from a steady cause and fixed character are more blamable or praiseworthy.
Beauty is no quality in things themselves; it exists merely in the mind which contemplates them.
Each mind perceives a different beauty; one person may even perceive deformity where another is sensible of beauty.
To seek in the nature of things for the real beauty or real deformity is as fruitless an inquiry as to pretend to ascertain the real sweet or the real bitter.
Beauty is such an order and construction of parts as, by the primary constitution of our nature, is fitted to give pleasure and satisfaction to the soul.
The rules of composition are not fixed by reasoning but are the same with those of mechanics or physics.
A standard of taste can be established by experience and the observation of the common sentiments of human nature.
Though a thousand different views may be taken, the general principles of taste are uniform in human nature.
Strong sense, united to delicate sentiment, improved by practice, perfected by comparison, and cleared of all prejudice, can give us the true standard of taste.
Beauty is immediately felt, not concluded from any chain of reasoning.
We call beautiful those proportions and appearances which are most useful and best adapted to the purpose for which they are intended.
Particular incidents and situations recall pleasure from memory and imagination, thereby increasing the perception of beauty.
Objects appear beautiful when they promise pleasure, either immediately or at a distance.
The sentiment of beauty and deformity must be entirely owing to ourselves, not to the objects.
In matters of taste, there is a considerable uniformity among men of the same country and the same age.
